why_correct: Claude optimizes for a well-formed, contextually appropriate response — and without an explicit format constraint, that means natural prose whose structure is determined by the input content, not by your parser's expectations. The failure mode is structural non-determinism: the model might lead with features one call, price the next, omit availability when the product description doesn't mention stock. The parser was written against the incidental structure of early test samples, not a guaranteed schema. Explicit format instructions — JSON with named keys, a fixed field order, constrained output shape — are the only way to make downstream parsing robust against this variance.

distractors.A: Claude has no session memory across API calls and no degradation mechanism tied to call volume. There is no internal state that 'wears out.' This would only be in play if you had evidence of model version changes or infrastructure-level issues, neither of which applies here.
distractors.C: The misconception is that the parser is the moving part. The parser isn't changing — Claude's output structure is. Retraining a parser on a non-deterministic output distribution just shifts the fragility; it doesn't eliminate it. The correct fix is constraining the source, not adapting the consumer.
distractors.D: Truncation is a real failure mode, but it produces consistent missing data — you'd see price and availability null from day one on long descriptions, not intermittent nulls appearing after a week of successful operation. The delayed onset and intermittency are the architectural tell that rule this out.

mental_model: Any time unstructured model output feeds a structured downstream consumer, treat format as a contract, not a convention. If the prompt doesn't specify the schema, the model owns the structure — and it will vary. The CCA heuristic: parser breaks in production on unchanged prompts → structural non-determinism, fix is explicit format instruction, not parser logic.
cross_domain_note: This is the same trust boundary issue that surfaces in agentic tool-use: if your orchestration layer parses model output to decide which tool to invoke, unspecified output format creates exactly the same silent misrouting risk at the agentic loop level.

why_correct: Claude is a stochastic system — even at low temperature, output structure varies across calls when unconstrained. The architectural principle is that any downstream parser is a contract, and contracts require deterministic interfaces. Explicit field labels and structure collapse Claude's structural variance to near-zero, making the prompt the schema definition for the integration layer. The 'natural writing' argument optimizes for human readability at the cost of machine parseability, which is the wrong tradeoff when the consumer is code.

distractors.A: Token count and response quality have no causal relationship in this direction — more tokens consumed doesn't improve quality, and format instructions don't meaningfully inflate token usage anyway. This conflates output length with output fidelity.
distractors.B: The failure mode here is assuming parsers can adapt to a moving target. In production, parsers are written once against an observed schema; if Claude's structure drifts across calls — different field ordering, label synonyms, added prose sections — the parser breaks silently or noisily. 'Adapting over time' describes a maintenance burden, not a feature.
distractors.D: This overstates the constraint. Claude doesn't default to a fixed format — it infers structure from context, which is precisely the problem. The issue isn't a hardcoded bullet-list default; it's unbounded structural variance, which is a harder failure to detect than a predictable wrong format.

mental_model: When the output consumer is a parser rather than a human, treat prompt format instructions as a schema contract, not a style preference. Any structural variance Claude is free to express becomes a potential parse failure in production. The CCA heuristic: if your downstream is code, explicit format spec is non-negotiable.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to agentic pipeline design — tool call outputs and orchestration layer handoffs have the same constraint. Structural inconsistency in an agentic loop compounds across steps, making format discipline upstream a reliability requirement, not a nicety.

why_correct: Claude's register and explanation depth are governed by the context it's given in the human/system turn — not inferred autonomously from content complexity. Explicit audience context is a first-class input: 'first-year law student with no prior IP exposure' directly scopes vocabulary, analogy density, and assumed priors in ways that implicit content signals cannot. Dynamic injection into a single canonical template gives you per-segment calibration without template sprawl, and keeps prompt iteration centralized. The architectural principle is that clarity about the intended recipient is part of the instruction set, not a nicety.

distractors.A: Two separate templates solve the immediate problem but introduce maintenance drift — when the core instruction evolves, you're now synchronizing two artifacts, and divergence is guaranteed over time. A is the right call only when the two segments require structurally different prompts (different tools, different output schemas, different grounding strategies), not just different register. Here, the delta is audience context, which parameterizes cleanly into one template.
distractors.C: This is the core misconception: that Claude performs implicit audience inference from content signals alone. In practice, a dense IP indemnification clause will produce a dense explanation regardless of who's reading it, because Claude has no signal about the reader — only about the clause. Relying on content complexity as a proxy for audience calibration produces exactly the churn described in the stem. The failure mode is inconsistent register that correlates with clause complexity rather than user expertise.
distractors.D: Post-processing for register is the wrong trust boundary — you're asking a second-pass transformation layer to reverse semantic decisions already baked into the generation. Simplification after the fact loses nuance; densification after the fact is nearly impossible without re-generation. This also doubles latency and token cost with no improvement to grounding quality.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic: if the output varies by who is reading it, that variance must be an explicit input — never an inferred one. When the only delta between user segments is audience context, parameterize a single template rather than forking it. Forks are justified by structural differences in output requirements, not by persona alone.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to the system prompt / human turn trust boundary design: audience context injected at the human turn is user-scoped and dynamic, while stable task framing lives in the system prompt — keeping the canonical instruction centralized while allowing per-request personalization without privilege escalation.

why_correct: Claude's register calibration is driven by whatever audience signal exists in the context window — with no signal, the model defaults to a statistically average register that's too dense for novices and too padded for experts. The lab result directly demonstrates this: the same base clause, unanchored to any segment, produced outputs that failed both rubrics simultaneously. Explicit audience context isn't stylistic polish; it's a load-bearing input that determines token-level choices around jargon density, hedge frequency, and explanation depth. The parameterized injection slot is the correct architectural pattern precisely because it supplies that signal without duplicating the rest of the template.

distractors.A: Role-play framing ('You are a legal tutor') can shift register, but the lab used audience description and it worked — the slot-filled condition scored well. The failure mode of A is conflating two valid mechanisms and asserting only one works. Audience description is often preferable to persona framing because it avoids the jailbreak surface that comes with deep role-play injection at the system prompt boundary.
distractors.C: This inverts causality. The rubrics are segment-specific because user needs are segment-specific — changing the measurement tool doesn't fix the underlying output mismatch. In production, a universal rubric would mask the register failure, not eliminate it, which is exactly the kind of eval debt that surfaces as user churn downstream.
distractors.D: The lab result actually vindicates shared templates with dynamic injection — the filled-slot condition performed well. Concluding that fully independent templates are required ignores the evidence and introduces unnecessary template sprawl, which multiplies maintenance surface and drift risk across segments.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic: Claude can't calibrate what it can't see. Any dimension of output you want controlled — register, depth, tone, assumed knowledge — needs an explicit signal in the context window. Dynamic injection slots are the canonical pattern for multi-segment systems because they centralize template logic while still delivering the per-request signal Claude needs.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to the cost optimization domain: a shared template with injection hits prompt caching on the static prefix, while per-segment templates forfeit that cache hit on every call — so the architecturally correct answer here is also the cheaper one at scale.

why_correct: In regulated deployment contexts, positive scope instructions create an implied boundary but leave Claude's behavior under ambiguity — the 9% drift is the empirical proof of that gap. The correct pattern is explicit negative constraints paired with scripted fallbacks, because the fallback solves two distinct problems simultaneously: it closes the generation ambiguity (Claude knows exactly what to output) and it satisfies compliance audit requirements for deterministic, reviewable deflection language. Regulators don't accept 'Claude usually refuses well' as a control; they need documented, reproducible response behavior. Named prohibited categories plus scripted fallbacks is the only architecture that produces an auditable, testable trust boundary.

distractors.A: Stronger positive scoping narrows the framing but doesn't change the fundamental mechanism — Claude is still inferring the boundary rather than receiving an explicit prohibition. The failure mode is that inference under ambiguous edge cases (e.g., a customer asks 'is my portfolio doing well?') still produces drift, just at a lower rate. A would be correct if the problem were purely about clarity of role framing with no regulatory audit trail requirement; it isn't.
distractors.C: The misconception is that explicit prohibition is sufficient and that Claude's refusal generation is reliable enough for a compliance context. The failure mode in production is that Claude's self-generated deflections will vary in tone, specificity, and occasionally accuracy — one session it deflects cleanly, another it partially engages before declining. You cannot pass a compliance audit with variable refusal phrasing. The scripted fallback is not verbosity; it's the control.
distractors.D: Post-processing filters treat the symptom, not the architecture. The prompt still generates off-topic content on every flagged request, meaning you're paying for hallucinated market commentary tokens and introducing latency from the filter layer. You've also created a silent failure surface: filter misses become undetected compliance violations with no prompt-layer backstop.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic for regulated deployments is: positive instructions define intent, negative constraints define the enforceable boundary, and scripted fallbacks are the audit artifact. When a compliance officer needs to show a regulator exactly what a customer received when Claude hit a prohibited topic, 'Claude generates an appropriate refusal' is not an answer — a logged, deterministic string is. Any time the stem mentions regulatory exposure or audit requirements, you need all three layers.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to the trust boundary design domain — the system prompt is your primary enforcement layer, and offloading boundary enforcement to model behavior alone is the same architectural error as offloading input validation to client-side code. Defense in depth requires the constraint to live at the layer closest to the generation decision.

why_correct: Negative constraints bound what Claude won't do, but they leave the generation of the refusal response itself in probabilistic space — Claude picks deflection language at inference time, and that language may inadvertently imply conditionality ('I can't answer that here' vs. 'I can only assist with account and transaction information'). The scripted fallback collapses that variance to a deterministic string, which has two compounding benefits: it closes the jailbreak surface created by open-ended refusal generation, and it creates an auditable artifact — compliance and legal can point to exact output text as evidence of controlled system behavior. In regulated financial services, that auditability is the architectural load-bearing element, not the UX polish.

distractors.A: UX cleanliness is a valid product consideration but not an architectural justification — it doesn't survive a compliance challenge or a security review. The failure mode is that 'cleaner style' doesn't explain why the scripted text is structurally necessary, so this answer gets cut when the reviewer pushes back with 'then rewrite it to sound nicer, still no script needed.'
distractors.C: This conflates two separate mechanisms: negative constraints do suppress out-of-scope responses with high reliability under normal conditions, and Claude doesn't systematically ignore them under user pressure in the way this option implies. The real problem isn't enforcement failure on refusals — it's non-determinism in the refusal's phrasing. Framing the fallback as 'the only reliable enforcement mechanism' overstates the failure mode and would justify scripting every output, which isn't the pattern.
distractors.D: FINRA Rule 2210 governs communications with the public around recommendations and disclosures — it doesn't mandate scripted AI fallback strings. This is a fabricated regulatory hook, and in an exam or production context, making architectural decisions on invented compliance mandates is a critical failure.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic: negative constraints control the decision boundary, but scripted responses control the output artifact. When the output itself is the compliance surface — subject to audit, litigation, or regulatory review — you cannot leave artifact generation to probabilistic sampling. Deterministic output = auditable evidence; model-generated output = probabilistic witness.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to trust boundary design in agentic systems: anywhere an action or output crosses into an external trust domain (regulator, auditor, end customer under fiduciary context), you want deterministic, reviewable artifacts — same reason you log exact tool call inputs/outputs rather than summarized intent.

why_correct: Claude's default completion behavior is to produce a coherent, bounded response — not to actively audit the input for all distinct intents before responding. Without explicit decomposition instructions, the model latches onto the most salient or first-encountered sub-question and generates a complete-feeling response around it, which satisfies its implicit 'respond helpfully' objective. The fix is structural: instruct the model to enumerate sub-questions before answering any of them, which forces the decomposition step into the reasoning chain explicitly. This is the 'explicit enumeration' pattern — Claude does not self-initiate structured multi-part handling without a prompt-level directive to do so.

distractors.A: A 40-token system prompt and a typical support ticket leave enormous headroom in any modern context window — truncation is not the mechanism here. Max_tokens would be the right lever if responses were getting cut off mid-sentence, not if entire sub-questions are silently omitted. The failure mode in A is misattributing a prompt-behavior gap to an infrastructure constraint.
distractors.C: The near-miss here is that 'prioritization' sounds like intentional behavior, but Claude isn't running a triage algorithm — it's just not enumerating. There's no urgency-ranking heuristic firing by default; the billing question likely appears first or is most syntactically prominent. Routing to humans is a valid escalation policy but doesn't fix the underlying decomposition gap when automation is appropriate.
distractors.D: Persona injection changes tone and role framing, not task decomposition logic. A 'helpful support agent' persona does not cause the model to enumerate sub-questions — that's a structural instruction gap, not an identity gap. In production, adding a persona without decomposition instructions produces the same partial response, just written in a warmer voice.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic: whenever multi-part input needs multi-part output, decomposition must be explicit in the prompt — Claude does not self-audit input structure by default. If you need enumerate-then-answer behavior, instruct it directly; implicit intent does not propagate into output structure.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to agentic loop design: in tool-use pipelines, you face the same issue if you don't explicitly instruct the orchestration layer to iterate over all sub-tasks before exiting — the agent will satisfy the first goal and terminate, not because it can't do the rest, but because it was never told to check.

why_correct: Token billing is based on tokens generated, not tokens delivered to the end user — the API charges for every output token Claude produces, regardless of what your post-processing layer strips before the customer sees it. The chain-of-thought enumeration still runs, still occupies the context window, and still appears on your invoice. The proposal therefore trades zero cost reduction for meaningful implementation complexity: you now own a fragile parsing/stripping layer that must handle edge cases in Claude's decomposition format. If the goal is clean responses with CoT-quality completeness, the correct architectural question is whether to use extended thinking, a two-pass design, or simply trust that well-structured direct instructions produce complete answers without visible enumeration.

distractors.A: The failure mode here is conflating 'not shown to user' with 'not billed.' Output tokens are counted at generation, not at delivery. A would be correct if APIs billed on streamed/delivered tokens rather than generated tokens — some internal tooling mental models leak this assumption, but it's not how Anthropic pricing works.
distractors.B: B correctly identifies that server-side vs. client-side stripping has latency implications, but it inherits A's core misconception that stripping affects token cost at all. It's a near-miss that adds a real engineering nuance on top of a false premise — the latency concern is valid in other contexts, but it doesn't rescue the proposal's cost-reduction rationale.
distractors.D: Chain-of-thought instructions are fully supported in the system prompt — placement in the human turn is not a requirement. This is a clearly-wrong distractor that would only apply if you were confusing system prompt scope with some instruction-following constraint that doesn't exist.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic for any 'strip it in post-processing to save cost' proposal is: cost is incurred at generation, not at presentation. If the tokens were generated, they were billed. Post-processing is a UX concern, not a cost-optimization lever.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to the cost optimization domain: the same principle applies to prompt caching — cache hits save on input tokens for repeated prefixes, but there's no analogous mechanism for output tokens. Knowing what the billing boundary is (input vs. output, generation vs. delivery) is foundational to any cost architecture.
